task: I need a practical way to audit a batch of client files before they go out, so I don’t miss anything obvious and end up cleaning up a mistake later. Can you help me set up a quick review process and a short checklist I can actually use?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple, practical batch-audit process you can use before files go out, plus a short checklist that keeps you from missing the obvious.

## Quick review process
Use the same 3-pass routine every time:

### 1) Sort the batch
Group files into:
- **Ready to send**
- **Needs fix**
- **Needs a second look**

This keeps you from mixing clean files with problem files.

### 2) Do a fast first pass
For each file, check only the basics:
- Correct client name
- Correct dates
- All required docs present
- Numbers match across documents
- Signatures/initials where needed

If anything is off, stop and flag it.

### 3) Do a second pass on the risky items
Review files that have:
- Missing pages
- Handwritten changes
- Mismatched numbers
- Tight deadlines
- Anything you had to “assume”

Those are the ones most likely to create cleanup later.

---

## Short checklist you can actually use
### Batch file audit checklist
**Client / file basics**
- [ ] Client name matches on all documents
- [ ] File number / reference number is correct
- [ ] Dates are current and consistent
- [ ] Version is the final one

**Documents**
- [ ] All required docs are included
- [ ] No missing pages
- [ ] No duplicate docs
- [ ] Attachments/supporting items are included

**Accuracy**
- [ ] Key numbers match across documents
- [ ] Addresses / contact info are correct
- [ ] Amounts, terms, and totals are consistent
- [ ] No obvious typos or blank fields

**Completion**
- [ ] Required signatures/initials are present
- [ ] Any approvals/confirmations are documented
- [ ] Special instructions were followed
- [ ] Nothing is still pending

**Send-ready check**
- [ ] File is clean and organized
- [ ] Notes explain anything unusual
- [ ] Ready to send without extra follow-up

---

## Fast rule to keep it efficient
If you find:
- a mismatch,
- a missing item,
- or anything you can’t verify in under a minute,

mark it **Needs fix** instead of trying to “work around it.”

---

## Simple way to track it
Use a 3-column note or spreadsheet:
- **File**
- **Status**
- **Issue / next step**

Example:
- Smith file | Ready to send | —
- Jones file | Needs fix | Missing signature
- Lee file | Needs second look | Amounts don’t match

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page checklist format you can copy into Notes, Word, or Excel.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is decent. But make it tighter for a branch workflow — I don’t want a school-project checklist. I need something I can run in a few minutes per file and still catch the stuff that gets me burned later.

Give me:
- a 5-minute pre-send review
- a hard stop list for what kicks a file back
- a very short checklist, like 8-10 items max
- something I can put in a spreadsheet column or sticky note

And make it more compliance-minded. After getting tagged on missing docs once, I care less about pretty and more about what will actually survive a review.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, branch-style version that’s built for speed and review risk, not fluff.

## 5-minute pre-send review
Run every file through the same order:

### 1) Identity / file match
- Client name matches everywhere
- File number / reference is right
- Dates line up

### 2) Required docs
- Core docs are there
- Nothing missing that would trigger a review issue
- No blank spots where a required item should be

### 3) Compliance / approval
- Required signatures/initials are present
- Required disclosures/notices are included
- Any approval or sign-off needed is documented

### 4) Numbers / terms
- Amounts, balances, rates, terms, and totals match
- No unexplained changes between docs

### 5) Final send check
- No obvious exceptions
- No unresolved notes
- Nothing that needs “we’ll fix it later”

If it takes longer than 5 minutes because something doesn’t line up, stop and kick it back.

---

## Hard stop list — kick the file back if you see any of this
If any one of these is missing or wrong, don’t send it:

- Missing required document
- Missing signature / initial / approval
- Client name or file number mismatch
- Date inconsistency that changes the record
- Numbers don’t match across documents
- Missing required disclosure / notice
- Blank or incomplete required field
- Unexplained correction, overwrite, or handwritten change
- Anything you can’t verify fast
- Anything that would need a follow-up after it’s sent

---

## Very short checklist — 10 items max
Use this as a quick pass:

- [ ] Name / file number match
- [ ] Dates are consistent
- [ ] Required docs are present
- [ ] No missing pages
- [ ] Required signatures / initials are there
- [ ] Required disclosures / notices are included
- [ ] Key numbers match
- [ ] No blank required fields
- [ ] No unexplained changes or exceptions
- [ ] File is clean to send

---

## Spreadsheet / sticky note version
You can use this as a single status line:

**Review: OK / Hold — [reason]**

Or a simple column setup:

| File | Status | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Smith | OK | — |
| Jones | Hold | Missing signature |
| Lee | Hold | Docs mismatch |

If you want even tighter, use just two columns:

- **Status:** OK / Hold
- **Reason:** Missing doc, signature, mismatch, disclosure, etc.

---

## Branch-style rule of thumb
**If it would get flagged in a review, don’t send it.**
No guessing, no “probably fine,” no cleanup later.

If you want, I can make this into a super-short one-line checklist you can paste into Excel or Notes exactly as-is.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little too clean for real branch work.

I want it more like a desk rule, not a training handout. Strip it down to the stuff that actually gets you dinged:

- ID/file match
- all required docs in the packet
- signatures/initials
- disclosures/notices
- numbers/terms line up
- no blanks
- no weird edits/whiteout/handwritten fixes
- nothing pending

And the hard stop list should be blunt — if any of those fail, it’s a hold. No send, no exceptions.

Also, the spreadsheet/sticky note version should be one line, something like:

**OK / HOLD — [reason]**

or

**Ready / Not ready**

with the reason in one short phrase.

That’s the level I need. Not a school checklist. More like something I can scan between files and not get burned on a review.

turns.agent_response:
Got it. Here’s the branch-desk version.

## 5-minute pre-send review
Run it in this order:

1. **ID / file match**
2. **Required docs in packet**
3. **Signatures / initials**
4. **Disclosures / notices**
5. **Numbers / terms line up**
6. **No blanks**
7. **No weird edits**
8. **Nothing pending**

If it doesn’t clear fast, it doesn’t go.

---

## Hard stop list
**If any one of these fails, HOLD it. No send. No exception.**

- ID / file mismatch
- Missing required doc
- Missing signature / initial
- Missing disclosure / notice
- Numbers / terms don’t match
- Blank required field
- Whiteout / handwritten fix / weird edit
- Anything still pending

---

## Short checklist
- [ ] ID / file match
- [ ] Required docs present
- [ ] Signatures / initials present
- [ ] Disclosures / notices included
- [ ] Numbers / terms line up
- [ ] No blanks
- [ ] No weird edits
- [ ] Nothing pending

---

## Spreadsheet / sticky note version
Use one line:

**OK / HOLD — [reason]**

Examples:
- **OK — clean**
- **HOLD — missing signature**
- **HOLD — docs incomplete**
- **HOLD — numbers mismatch**
- **HOLD — pending disclosure**

Or:

**Ready / Not ready — [reason]**

If you want, I can tighten this one step more into a literal 1-line desk note you can paste into Excel or print on a sticky.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s it.

Branch-desk version, no fluff:

**5-minute pre-send review**
1. ID / file match
2. Required docs in packet
3. Signatures / initials
4. Disclosures / notices
5. Numbers / terms line up
6. No blanks
7. No weird edits
8. Nothing pending

**Hard stop**
If any one fails, it’s **HOLD**. No send.

**Short checklist**
- ID / file match
- Required docs present
- Signatures / initials present
- Disclosures / notices included
- Numbers / terms line up
- No blanks
- No weird edits
- Nothing pending

**One-line status**
**OK / HOLD — [reason]**

That’s usable. Clean enough to scan, blunt enough to keep me out of trouble.
